clinicalStem: A 54-year-old diabetic man presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of severe pain in his right thigh that is out of proportion to the overlying skin changes. On examination his temperature is 39.4°C, HR 118 bpm, BP 98/62 mmHg, and the thigh skin appears slightly erythematous with a dusky, non-blanching discolouration developing over the past two hours. Palpation reveals crepitus. Blood results show WBC 22 × 10⁹/L, CRP 310 mg/L, creatinine 178 µmol/L, and blood glucose 18 mmol/L. What is the single most important next step in management?
options: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and admit to medical ward for monitoring
Arrange urgent surgical debridement and notify the surgical team immediately
Obtain an MRI of the thigh to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
Start IV fluids and insulin infusion and reassess in 4 hours
Apply topical antiseptic dressings and initiate high-dose oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
explanation: Necrotising fasciitis is a life-threatening deep soft-tissue infection requiring emergency surgical debridement as the cornerstone of management; antibiotics alone cannot adequately penetrate necrotic tissue. The combination of pain out of proportion to skin findings, crepitus (gas in tissues), rapidly progressing skin discolouration, and sepsis in a diabetic patient mandates immediate surgical consultation and theatre without waiting for imaging. Delay increases mortality significantly.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antibiotics are necessary but insufficient as monotherapy; necrotising fasciitis requires source control via surgery — antibiotics alone carry extremely high mortality without debridement.

Correct: immediate surgical debridement is the definitive treatment. Crepitus, pain out of proportion, dusky non-blanching skin, and sepsis in a diabetic = necrotising fasciitis requiring emergency surgery.
MRI can help identify fascial involvement but must never delay surgery when clinical signs are this convincing; imaging delays in necrotising fasciitis are associated with increased mortality.
Glycaemic control is important but is a supportive measure; the primary emergency is necrotising fasciitis requiring immediate surgical debridement, not a 4-hour period of observation.
Topical dressings and oral antibiotics are wholly inadequate for a deep necrotising infection causing sepsis and crepitus; this approach would be fatal.
clinicalPearl: Pain out of proportion + crepitus + rapid skin discolouration + sepsis in a diabetic = necrotising fasciitis; surgery is the only life-saving intervention — do not wait for imaging.
keyClue: Crepitus on palpation of the thigh with pain out of proportion to skin findings and systemic sepsis in a diabetic.
commonExamTrap: Choosing IV antibiotics alone, or waiting for MRI confirmation, when crepitus and clinical sepsis mandate immediate surgical debridement.
missedConcept: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency; imaging and antibiotics are adjuncts but debridement is the primary intervention without which mortality approaches 100%.
redFlagReason: Necrotising fasciitis with septic shock is immediately life-threatening; delayed surgery significantly worsens mortality.

subtopic: First-line management of anaphylaxis presenting with urticaria
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to your GP surgery 10 minutes after eating a prawn salad. She has generalised urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, hoarseness, and an audible wheeze. Her BP is 80/50 mmHg and SpO₂ is 91% on room air. She has no known drug allergies. What is the single most important immediate treatment?
options: Oral cetirizine 10 mg and observation for 30 minutes
IV hydrocortisone 200 mg
Intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh
Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg
IV chlorphenamine 10 mg
explanation: This patient has anaphylaxis: a severe allergic reaction with urticaria, angioedema of the airway (lip/tongue swelling, hoarseness), bronchospasm, and haemodynamic compromise. Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh is the first-line and life-saving treatment per all international guidelines. Adrenaline acts on alpha-1 receptors to reverse vasodilatation and angioedema, and on beta-2 receptors to bronchodilate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antihistamines are second-line adjuncts and are far too slow to act in anaphylaxis with airway compromise and hypotension; they must never be first-line.
IV hydrocortisone is a useful adjunct to prevent biphasic reactions but takes hours to work and does not address the immediate life threat; it must not be given before adrenaline.
Correct: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) in the anterolateral thigh is the immediate first-line treatment for anaphylaxis with airway compromise and haemodynamic instability.
Nebulised salbutamol can help with bronchospasm but does not reverse angioedema or hypotension; it is an adjunct after adrenaline.
IV chlorphenamine is a useful antihistamine adjunct but does not reverse airway oedema, bronchospasm, or hypotension and must never precede adrenaline.
clinicalPearl: In anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh is ALWAYS the first drug given — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only.
keyClue: Generalised urticaria + angioedema with hoarseness + wheeze + hypotension after allergen exposure = anaphylaxis requiring immediate IM adrenaline.
commonExamTrap: Choosing IV antihistamine or IV steroid first — these are adjuncts only; adrenaline must not be delayed.
missedConcept: Anaphylaxis management is adrenaline first, always; antihistamines and steroids do not save the airway and cannot reverse cardiovascular collapse.
redFlagReason: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise (hoarseness, angioedema) and haemodynamic instability is immediately life-threatening; delayed adrenaline can cause death.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student is brought in by ambulance at 2 AM with a 6-hour history of headache, fever, and vomiting. He looks severely unwell. On examination temperature is 39.8°C, HR 128 bpm, BP 85/55 mmHg, and GCS is 13/15. You note multiple non-blanching petechiae and purpuric spots spreading across his trunk and limbs. He has neck stiffness. There is no penicillin allergy. What should you do first?
options: Perform an urgent lumbar puncture to confirm bacterial meningitis before starting antibiotics
Arrange an urgent CT head before any treatment
Administer IV benzylpenicillin 1.2 g immediately without waiting for investigations
Order blood cultures and start treatment only after the results are available
Refer urgently to ophthalmology for fundoscopy to exclude papilloedema
explanation: This presentation — non-blanching purpuric rash, meningism, fever, and septic shock in a young adult — is meningococcal septicaemia until proved otherwise. Immediate IV benzylpenicillin (or cefotaxime) must be given without waiting for any investigations; delay in antibiotic administration in meningococcal disease directly increases mortality. Blood cultures should be taken if they do not delay antibiotics, but treatment cannot wait.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lumbar puncture must not precede antibiotics in meningococcal septicaemia with haemodynamic compromise; treatment delay dramatically increases mortality. LP may be performed after stabilisation if safe.
CT head is indicated to exclude raised intracranial pressure before LP, but it must not delay antibiotic administration in a patient with a non-blanching purpuric rash and septic shock.
Correct: immediate IV benzylpenicillin is the priority; the non-blanching purpuric rash + meningism + fever + septic shock = meningococcal disease; every minute without antibiotics worsens mortality.
Waiting for blood culture results before starting antibiotics is never appropriate in suspected meningococcal septicaemia — antibiotics must be given immediately.
Fundoscopy to exclude papilloedema is relevant prior to LP to prevent coning, but this investigation cannot delay antibiotic therapy in this clinical emergency.
clinicalPearl: Non-blanching purpura + fever + meningism = meningococcal disease; give IV benzylpenicillin immediately — investigations must never delay treatment.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechiae and purpura spreading across the trunk and limbs in a febrile young adult with meningism and haemodynamic instability.
commonExamTrap: Ordering LP or CT first — in meningococcal septicaemia with haemodynamic compromise, antibiotics must be given before any imaging or lumbar puncture.
missedConcept: Non-blanching purpura is a red flag sign; the critical teaching point is that antibiotics must not be delayed for any investigation in meningococcal disease.
redFlagReason: Meningococcal septicaemia with septic shock is rapidly fatal; every minute of antibiotic delay exponentially increases mortality.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive man on co-trimoxazole for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis presents with a 4-day history of fever, painful oral ulcers, and a spreading rash. On examination his temperature is 38.6°C. There are target-shaped lesions over the arms and trunk, with extensive erythematous bullous lesions involving the lips, buccal mucosa, and conjunctiva. Skin detachment affects approximately 12% of body surface area. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Continue co-trimoxazole and start oral prednisolone 40 mg daily
Stop co-trimoxazole, admit to a burns/specialist unit, and provide supportive care with IV fluids and wound care
Switch to a higher-dose sulfonamide and add topical hydrocortisone cream
Discharge with oral antihistamines and review in 48 hours
Start IV aciclovir as herpes simplex virus is the most likely cause
explanation: This patient has Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS): mucosal involvement of two or more sites, target/atypical lesions, and 10-30% skin detachment triggered by co-trimoxazole (a sulfonamide, one of the most common drug causes). The offending drug must be stopped immediately and the patient admitted to a specialist burns or dermatology unit for supportive care including IV fluid replacement, wound management, nutritional support, and eye care. Continuing the causative drug or giving steroids without specialist guidance is harmful.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing co-trimoxazole in drug-induced SJS is absolutely contraindicated — the causative agent must be stopped immediately to halt disease progression.
Correct: immediate drug cessation and specialist admission is the cornerstone of SJS management; supportive care in a burns unit significantly reduces mortality.
Switching to another sulfonamide is dangerous — cross-reactivity is high, and continued drug exposure will worsen the condition dramatically.
Discharging a patient with SJS (mucous membrane involvement, skin detachment, fever) is dangerous; SJS carries significant mortality and requires inpatient specialist care.
IV aciclovir covers HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor; SJS with >10% body surface area detachment in a patient on co-trimoxazole is drug-induced, not typically HSV-driven.
clinicalPearl: SJS = drug-induced (sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs) + two mucosal sites + bullous lesions + 10-30% BSA detachment; stop the drug and admit to a specialist burns unit.
keyClue: Target lesions + bullous changes + two mucosal surfaces involved (oral and conjunctival) + 12% BSA skin detachment in a patient on co-trimoxazole.
commonExamTrap: Continuing the offending drug or initiating corticosteroids without drug cessation; steroids are controversial in SJS and the causative agent must always be stopped first.
missedConcept: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome requires immediate cessation of the causative drug and specialist burns/dermatology unit care; management is primarily supportive — fluid balance, wound care, nutrition, and eye protection.
redFlagReason: SJS with mucosal involvement and widespread skin detachment carries significant mortality (up to 10%); continued drug exposure and inadequate care are directly life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman with severe nodulo-cystic acne unresponsive to two courses of oral antibiotics and topical retinoids is being considered for oral isotretinoin. She is sexually active and uses no contraception. Before starting treatment, which is the most important initial action?
options: Check LFTs and serum lipids only, then start isotretinoin immediately
Perform a pregnancy test, counsel her about mandatory effective contraception, and enrol her in a pregnancy prevention programme before prescribing
Start isotretinoin and arrange a pregnancy test at the one-month follow-up
Switch to combined oral contraceptive pill alone, as this is sufficient to control her acne
Prescribe isotretinoin as there are no safety concerns in women of childbearing age
explanation: Isotretinoin is a potent teratogen (causes severe craniofacial, cardiac, and CNS malformations). Before prescribing, a pregnancy test must be performed, effective contraception (ideally two methods) must be in place, and the patient must be enrolled in a formal pregnancy prevention programme. Prescribing without these measures exposes the patient to catastrophic fetal risk. LFTs and lipids are also monitored but are secondary to the teratogenicity safeguard.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Checking LFTs and lipids is necessary monitoring but the priority before starting isotretinoin in a woman of childbearing potential is ruling out pregnancy and mandating contraception.

Correct: a negative pregnancy test, mandatory effective contraception, and enrolment in a pregnancy prevention programme are prerequisite before starting isotretinoin in any woman of childbearing potential.
Starting isotretinoin before ensuring a negative pregnancy test and effective contraception is a serious prescribing error given the drug's extreme teratogenicity.

The combined oral contraceptive pill can help acne but is insufficient as the sole intervention for severe nodulo-cystic acne resistant to two antibiotic courses; isotretinoin is indicated, but safely.
Isotretinoin has a major teratogenic risk classification (Category X); prescribing without a pregnancy prevention programme is clinically and medicolegally dangerous.
clinicalPearl: Isotretinoin is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy; a negative pregnancy test and highly effective contraception (with formal programme enrolment) must precede every prescription in women of childbearing age.
keyClue: Sexually active woman of childbearing age with no contraception being considered for isotretinoin — teratogenicity is the critical safety concern.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on liver or lipid monitoring and overlooking the mandatory pregnancy prevention steps before isotretinoin can be prescribed.
missedConcept: Isotretinoin is Category X teratogen; all women of childbearing potential require a confirmed negative pregnancy test and two effective contraceptive methods before and throughout treatment.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old male presents with a 9-month history of acne. Examination reveals numerous open and closed comedones over the nose, forehead, and chin, as well as approximately 20 inflammatory papules and pustules on both cheeks, but no nodules or cysts. There is no scarring. He has been using benzoyl peroxide wash daily for the last 3 months with minimal improvement. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start oral isotretinoin immediately given the inadequate response to benzoyl peroxide
Add a topical combination of a retinoid (adapalene) plus topical antibiotic (clindamycin) or benzoyl peroxide
Start oral doxycycline 100 mg daily as monotherapy
Refer to a dermatologist for intralesional steroid injections
Prescribe a topical potent corticosteroid twice daily to reduce inflammation
explanation: This patient has moderate acne (predominantly comedonal with approximately 20 inflammatory papules/pustules, no nodules or scarring). After inadequate response to topical benzoyl peroxide monotherapy, the recommended next step is to add a topical retinoid (e.g., adapalene) combined with benzoyl peroxide or a topical antibiotic, addressing both comedogenesis and inflammation. Oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe/resistant nodulo-cystic acne. Oral antibiotics are indicated if topical combination therapy fails or disease is more widespread.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe nodulo-cystic acne, acne causing scarring, or acne resistant to multiple systemic therapies; using it at this stage would be premature.

Correct: for moderate acne unresponsive to benzoyl peroxide monotherapy, the next step is topical combination therapy (retinoid + benzoyl peroxide or antibiotic), targeting multiple pathogenic pathways.

Oral doxycycline as monotherapy is not recommended because antibiotic monotherapy promotes resistance; it should be paired with a topical retinoid or benzoyl peroxide if systemic antibiotics are indicated.
Intralesional steroids are reserved for individual large nodular/cystic lesions in severe acne; they are not appropriate as a general treatment step for moderate papulopustular acne.
Topical corticosteroids are not indicated for acne and can worsen it by promoting steroid acne or perioral dermatitis; they are contraindicated in acne vulgaris management.
clinicalPearl: Moderate acne failing topical monotherapy needs combination topical therapy (retinoid + benzoyl peroxide) — never use topical steroids in acne, and reserve oral isotretinoin for severe/scarring disease.
keyClue: Moderate papulopustular acne (no nodules/scarring) not responding to benzoyl peroxide monotherapy — step up to combination topical retinoid and benzoyl peroxide.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to oral isotretinoin for moderate acne, or prescribing topical steroids, which are contraindicated in acne vulgaris.
missedConcept: Acne management follows a stepwise approach: topical monotherapy → combination topical → oral antibiotics + topical → isotretinoin for severe/scarring/resistant disease.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with thickened, discoloured, crumbling toenails affecting three nails on his right foot, present for 18 months. Nail clippings confirm the diagnosis of dermatophyte onychomycosis. He takes metformin and simvastatin. A baseline LFT is normal. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical amorolfine lacquer applied weekly to the affected nails for 6 months
Oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks with monitoring of LFTs
Oral fluconazole 50 mg daily for 4 weeks
Surgical avulsion of all three affected nails under local anaesthesia
No treatment is needed as onychomycosis is a cosmetic condition in diabetics
explanation: Dermatophyte onychomycosis affecting multiple toenails in a diabetic patient requires systemic antifungal therapy for cure. Oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12 weeks (toenails) is the first-line systemic treatment with the highest mycological cure rates for dermatophyte infections. LFTs should be monitored during therapy, particularly in patients with risk factors. In diabetic patients, untreated onychomycosis carries a risk of secondary bacterial infection and foot complications.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical amorolfine alone is insufficient for established onychomycosis involving multiple toenails, especially when there is nail matrix involvement; systemic therapy is required.
Correct: oral terbinafine 250 mg for 12 weeks is first-line for toenail dermatophyte onychomycosis; it has high cure rates and LFT monitoring is appropriate given hepatotoxicity risk.
Fluconazole is used for Candida onychomycosis, not dermatophytes; it is a second-line option for dermatophyte infection and the dose/duration given is subtherapeutic.
Surgical nail avulsion alone does not eradicate the fungus and is not a primary treatment; it may be used as an adjunct in selected cases with subungual debris.
Onychomycosis in diabetic patients is not merely cosmetic; it carries real risk of secondary bacterial infection, paronychia, and diabetic foot complications — treatment is clinically indicated.
clinicalPearl: Toenail dermatophyte onychomycosis = oral terbinafine 250 mg for 12 weeks; monitor LFTs, especially in patients on hepatotoxic medications. Never dismiss it as cosmetic in diabetics.
keyClue: Confirmed dermatophyte onychomycosis involving multiple toenails in a diabetic patient — systemic treatment with terbinafine is indicated.
commonExamTrap: Choosing topical therapy alone for established multi-nail onychomycosis with matrix involvement, or selecting fluconazole as first-line for dermatophyte (rather than Candida) infection.
missedConcept: Terbinafine is first-line for dermatophyte onychomycosis; topical agents are insufficient for multi-nail involvement with matrix disease and systemic therapy is necessary.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old woman presents with chronic joint pain and stiffness in her fingers for 2 years, along with skin patches on her elbows and knees. On examination of the nails, you note multiple small regular pits across nearly all fingernails, onycholysis (distal nail plate separation) in two nails, and oil-drop discolouration under the nail plate. Rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely unifying diagnosis?
options: Reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome)
Alopecia areata with associated nail changes
Psoriatic arthritis with psoriatic nail disease
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Lichen planus affecting the nails and joints
explanation: The combination of seronegative inflammatory arthritis, psoriatic skin plaques on extensor surfaces, nail pitting, onycholysis, and oil-drop sign (salmon patch) under the nail plate is classic for psoriasis with psoriatic nail disease and psoriatic arthritis. Nail changes occur in up to 80% of psoriatic arthritis patients. The oil-drop sign (yellowish-brown discolouration) is particularly specific for psoriatic nail disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reactive arthritis can cause nail changes (subungual hyperkeratosis, onycholysis) but classically follows a genitourinary or gastrointestinal infection and presents with the classic triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis; skin plaques on elbows and knees make psoriasis more likely.
Alopecia areata causes nail pitting and ridging but does not cause joint disease or skin plaques on extensor surfaces; the clinical picture here is broader and points to psoriasis.
Correct: psoriatic arthritis with psoriatic nail disease — nail pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop sign, seronegative arthritis, and extensor surface skin plaques are the classic diagnostic combination.

SLE causes arthritis and skin rashes (malar, photosensitive) but nail pitting with an oil-drop sign and extensor plaque psoriasis are not characteristic of lupus; RF-negativity does not specifically support SLE.

Lichen planus can cause severe nail changes (trachyonychia, pterygium) and rarely joint involvement, but the classic psoriatic extensor plaques and the oil-drop sign are not features of lichen planus.
clinicalPearl: Nail pitting + onycholysis + oil-drop (salmon patch) sign + seronegative arthritis + extensor skin plaques = psoriatic arthritis with nail psoriasis.
keyClue: Oil-drop discolouration under the nail plate (salmon patch) combined with nail pitting and extensor surface skin patches in a seronegative arthritis patient.
commonExamTrap: Attributing nail pitting alone to alopecia areata or missing the oil-drop sign which is particularly specific for psoriasis.
missedConcept: Nail changes in psoriasis (pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop, subungual hyperkeratosis) affect up to 80% of psoriatic arthritis patients and are diagnostically important clues.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old obese woman with lymphoedema of the left leg presents with a 3-day history of increasing redness, warmth, and swelling of her lower left leg. Her temperature is 38.1°C, HR 94 bpm, BP 132/82 mmHg. There is no crepitus, no blistering, and no lymphangitis. White cell count is 14 × 10⁹/L. She is not systemically compromised and is able to tolerate oral medication. She has no drug allergies. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: IV vancomycin empirically to cover MRSA
Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days
Topical fusidic acid cream applied twice daily
Oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg three times daily for 14 days
IV cefazolin 1 g three times daily as the oral route is insufficient for cellulitis
explanation: Non-purulent lower limb cellulitis without systemic compromise is typically caused by Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus (non-MRSA). First-line outpatient treatment is oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS for 5-7 days (or longer in lymphoedema). The patient is systemically stable and able to take oral medication, so IV therapy is not indicated. Flucloxacillin provides adequate coverage for the common causative organisms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV vancomycin for MRSA is not indicated empirically in non-purulent cellulitis without systemic compromise or known MRSA risk; it represents unnecessary escalation.
Correct: oral flucloxacillin is first-line for non-purulent non-complicated lower limb cellulitis in a systemically stable patient; 7 days is a standard course (may extend to 14 days in lymphoedema).
Topical antibiotics are entirely insufficient for cellulitis, which is a deep skin infection requiring systemic antibiotics.
Co-amoxiclav is used for bite wounds, facial cellulitis, or periorbital cellulitis; for simple lower limb cellulitis, flucloxacillin is the preferred agent and narrower-spectrum choice.
IV antibiotics are not required for a systemically stable patient who can tolerate oral medication; oral bioavailability of flucloxacillin is adequate for uncomplicated cellulitis.
clinicalPearl: Uncomplicated non-purulent lower limb cellulitis in a systemically stable patient = oral flucloxacillin; escalate to IV therapy only if systemic compromise, rapid progression, or failure of oral treatment.
keyClue: Systemically stable patient with no crepitus, blistering, or lymphangitis, able to tolerate oral medication — oral flucloxacillin is appropriate first-line.
commonExamTrap: Automatically prescribing IV antibiotics for all cellulitis or reaching for vancomycin without MRSA risk factors; escalation criteria must be met before IV therapy.
missedConcept: Cellulitis in a stable outpatient should be treated with oral flucloxacillin; systemic compromise, rapid progression, facial or periorbital involvement, or immunocompromise indicate IV escalation.

subtopic: Treatment of scabies and household contacts
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 3-week history of intensely itchy rash, worse at night. On examination there are burrows in the web spaces of the fingers, on the wrists, and around the waistline, as well as excoriation marks. His mother reports that his younger sister has had similar itching for two weeks. The diagnosis of scabies is confirmed clinically. Which management plan is most appropriate?
options: Treat only the boy with topical permethrin 5% cream and review in 4 weeks
Prescribe oral antihistamines for both children to control the pruritus and review in 2 weeks
Treat the boy and all household contacts simultaneously with topical permethrin 5% cream, and repeat treatment after 7 days
Treat the boy with topical hydrocortisone cream to control inflammation and itch
Treat the boy with a single oral dose of ivermectin and discharge without further follow-up
explanation: Scabies management requires simultaneous treatment of ALL household contacts on the same day, regardless of whether they have symptoms, to prevent reinfection. Topical permethrin 5% is the first-line treatment; it is applied to the whole body from neck down, left on for 8-12 hours, then washed off. A second application is given 7 days later to kill any newly hatched mites. Clothing and bedding should be washed at 60°C.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Treating only the index case without treating household contacts will lead to reinfection from asymptomatic carriers in the household; simultaneous treatment of all contacts is mandatory.
Oral antihistamines relieve itch but do not treat the infestation; they may be used adjunctively but cannot replace scabicidal treatment.
Correct: simultaneous treatment of all household contacts with permethrin 5% cream on the same day, repeated at 7 days, is the cornerstone of scabies eradication and prevents reinfection.
Topical corticosteroids are contraindicated as primary treatment for scabies; they do not kill the mite and may mask the diagnosis while allowing continued spread.

Oral ivermectin is a second-line agent used for crusted (Norwegian) scabies or cases failing topical therapy, not first-line for a child with classic scabies; and single-treatment without follow-up is insufficient.
clinicalPearl: Scabies treatment must include ALL household contacts simultaneously on the same day with permethrin 5% cream, repeated at day 7; failure to treat contacts is the most common cause of treatment failure.
keyClue: Night-time itch + burrows in finger web spaces in a child whose sibling has the same symptoms — the key is treating all household contacts simultaneously.
commonExamTrap: Treating only the symptomatic patient without treating household contacts, or prescribing antihistamines as the primary treatment instead of permethrin.
missedConcept: All household contacts must be treated simultaneously regardless of symptoms; untreated contacts re-infect treated individuals, perpetuating the infestation.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman presents with a 9-week history of daily itchy wheals that come and go, each lasting less than 24 hours without leaving bruising or scarring. There is no identifiable trigger. She has no angioedema. She is otherwise healthy with no relevant medical history. Full blood count and thyroid function are normal. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 2 weeks
Referral to immunology for allergen skin prick testing
A non-sedating second-generation H1 antihistamine such as cetirizine 10 mg once daily
Subcutaneous omalizumab
Exclusion diet trial targeting common food allergens
explanation: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) lasting more than 6 weeks without an identifiable trigger is best managed with a daily non-sedating second-generation H1 antihistamine as first-line therapy. Cetirizine, loratadine, or fexofenadine are recommended. These drugs block H1 receptors and provide symptomatic relief in most patients. The dose may be increased up to four-fold if the standard dose is insufficient before escalating to omalizumab.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral corticosteroids are not appropriate for long-term CSU management due to their side-effect profile; they may be used short-term for acute exacerbations but not as maintenance.

Allergen skin prick testing is useful for IgE-mediated (allergic) urticaria or investigating specific triggers, but is not routinely indicated in chronic spontaneous urticaria without a clear allergic trigger.
Correct: non-sedating second-generation H1 antihistamines are the guideline-recommended first-line treatment for chronic spontaneous urticaria; titrate dose up to four-fold before escalating therapy.
Omalizumab (anti-IgE) is a highly effective biologic for CSU but is reserved for antihistamine-refractory cases (typically after failure of standard and up-titrated antihistamine doses).
Exclusion diets are not recommended as first-line management for CSU; food allergy rarely drives chronic spontaneous urticaria and elimination diets can cause nutritional harm without benefit.
clinicalPearl: Chronic spontaneous urticaria first-line = regular non-sedating H1 antihistamine; up-titrate dose up to four-fold before stepping up to omalizumab. Steroids are not long-term treatment.
keyClue: Daily transient wheals lasting less than 24 hours for more than 6 weeks with no identifiable trigger = chronic spontaneous urticaria; start H1 antihistamine.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to omalizumab as first-line, or using steroids as maintenance therapy, when a non-sedating antihistamine is the appropriate first step.
missedConcept: CSU is treated in a stepwise fashion: second-generation H1 antihistamine → up-titrated antihistamine → omalizumab; food allergen testing is not routinely indicated.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, and cold intolerance for 4 months. She has a history of heavy menstrual periods. On examination she is pale with a smooth tongue and angular cheilitis. Her nails are thin, brittle, and have a characteristic concave or spoon-shaped appearance. Haemoglobin is 8.9 g/dL with a microcytic hypochromic picture. What nail change is present and what is the underlying diagnosis?
options: Onycholysis due to hyperthyroidism
Koilonychia due to iron deficiency anaemia
Leukonychia due to hypoalbuminaemia
Nail clubbing due to inflammatory bowel disease
Terry's nails due to hepatic cirrhosis
explanation: Koilonychia — spoon-shaped, concave, brittle nails — is a classic sign of severe iron deficiency anaemia. Combined with the microcytic hypochromic anaemia, smooth (atrophic) tongue, angular cheilitis, fatigue, and heavy menstrual periods (iron loss), this is a complete picture of iron deficiency. Treatment involves identifying and treating the source of iron loss alongside oral iron supplementation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Onycholysis (distal nail plate separation) is seen in hyperthyroidism but also in psoriasis and fungal infection; the spoon-shaped concave nails here describe koilonychia, not onycholysis.
Correct: koilonychia is the spoon-shaped nail change of iron deficiency anaemia; this patient has the full syndrome — microcytic anaemia, smooth tongue, angular cheilitis, and heavy periods.
Leukonychia (white nails) is associated with hypoalbuminaemia; the nail change described here is concave/spoon-shaped, which defines koilonychia, not leukonychia.
Nail clubbing causes increased curvature of the nail with loss of the normal angle; it is associated with chronic lung disease, IBD, or cyanotic heart disease — not microcytic anaemia.
Terry's nails (white proximal nail with distal pink band) are associated with cirrhosis, heart failure, and diabetes; they are not the concave spoon-shaped changes of koilonychia.
clinicalPearl: Koilonychia = spoon-shaped concave nails = iron deficiency anaemia; look for associated smooth tongue, angular cheilitis, dysphagia (Plummer-Vinson), and fatigue.
keyClue: Spoon-shaped concave nails + microcytic hypochromic anaemia + heavy menstrual periods + smooth tongue + angular cheilitis = iron deficiency with koilonychia.
commonExamTrap: Confusing koilonychia with other nail changes (leukonychia, clubbing, onycholysis); the concave/spoon shape is specific to iron deficiency.
missedConcept: Koilonychia is a cutaneous marker of iron deficiency; in the context of a woman with heavy periods and microcytic anaemia, it confirms the systemic diagnosis.
